title: Lesson 436
transcript: We just introduced the concepts of data remnants and data destruction. Let's get into a little more detail in relation to this. So as part of the data lifecycle, we obviously want to protect data as it goes through its lifecycle focused on its value. That's always what drives how we protect things. That includes data. But at the end of the data lifecycle is what we call the destruction phase. And that has to be done properly based on the value as well. So imagine something that has a lot of value but needs to be destroyed. It needs to be destroyed in such a way so that it cannot be reconstructed by anyone else, for example. The data destruction phase needs to be done properly, and that's what we're going to talk about here in this section. And data destruction methods and data remnants, these are terms that you need to remember, are related to each other. And so the way that we destroy data needs to be good enough in order to prevent data remnants, which is defined as data remaining on an object after you've tried to securely get rid of it. There's different methods that we can employ here to be able to securely get rid of data. First of all, a definition of what data remnants is, and if you focus on the word remnants, I think it tells you what we're talking about here. We want to prevent data remaining on an object based on its value. So there's different methods that we can use here. But here's a good industry definition data remaining after deletion or secure deletion, for example, based on the value. of that data. Now, I think we've already stressed this many times. Obviously the owners are always accountable for protecting the value of their assets. And so data owners are accountable for protecting the value of the data that they own on behalf of the organization. But that also includes the destruction right so if we're trying to prevent data remaining on an object so that it cannot be reconstructed after we've destroyed it owners are also accountable for that right so destruction methods is that something that ultimately the owners are accountable for And need to be supported by security and technology, of course, so that we can do it based on the value of the data at the end of its life cycle. All right. So this comes from the industry now. Again, it's not ISE 2 that just dreams up certain terminologies and so on. Anything that is addressed in the exam First of all, comes from the exam outline, but the exam outline is derived from the common body of knowledge of security. So it's the industry, for example, that's advocating the different methods here for data destruction. And we're going to try and make it as easy as possible here because that's what's required as far as the exam is concerned. There's really three categories of how we can prevent data remnants or ensure the secure destruction of data at the end of its life cycle. And these are the three categories that you see here. So we've ordered them by order of best to worst. So your first option, best option, if it's available, is destruction. And here's the perfect definition from an industry perspective, common body of knowledge perspective. of what destruction really means what you're trying to do here is you're destroying the media that that data is on because if you destroy the the media obviously you're making the media unusable but you're also destroying the data that was on that media This is your best option always if it's available. You're destroying, physically destroying the media so that anything that's on there from a data perspective is also destroyed and not available for reconstruction in any way. Now, if destruction is not an option, your next best option is what we call purging. And here's the industry definition that you should remember of purging. We're using some sort of method that will ensure destruction. that that data cannot be reconstructed by any known means. So the industry does use the word cannot. So if destruction is not available, your next best option is to purge. And purge means exactly this. You're using some sort of technique that obviously will ensure that that data cannot be reconstructed by any known means. Now, if purging is not an option, then the last option that's available for us is what we call clearing. And here's the industry definition of clearing. It's almost the same as purging, except instead of using the word cannot, we use the word may not. So we're using some sort of method that may not allow the reconstruction of that data so that doesn't sound very good and in fact it isn't now how does this relate to some of the methods that you may have heard of right because we've all heard of different ways to try and get rid of data and here are those categories that we just described at the bottom physical destruction of the media purging which again meant that we're using some sort of method that will ensure that that data cannot be reconstructed by any known means And then, of course, the last option, which we said wasn't very good, is clearing where the data may or may not be reconstructed. All right, so here are some of those Methods that would fit in these categories. All right now obviously media destruction and doing things like incinerating or turning your hard drive into liquid by using high heat and You've heard of some of these too, shredding, disintegrating, drilling a hole through your hard drive, for example. These are all destruction methods. And as far as the exam is concerned, we need to be very careful here because a lot of us have a tendency to overthink and look at things too deeply. Because some of you may be looking at these methods that we've put in this category here, that the industry has put in these categories, in this category of destruction here. And you might be thinking, well, you know, turning something into liquid is better than, for example, drilling a hole through the hard drive. And you'd be absolutely correct in thinking that. But as far as the exam is concerned, no need to get to that level of detail. All right. Any destruction method is obviously fitting here in the destruction category. It's better than anything else. The exam is not going to ask you to understand the difference between drilling and incinerating and disintegrating and treading. As long as the method is destruction, it fits here. It's what we prefer. Obviously, if it's available, destruction methods is what we want because it makes the media unusable, but also gets rid of the data that was on that media. Now, if destruction is not an option, we said purging was next. But your next option here, as far as methods are concerned, specific methods, is what we call degaussing. And you may recognize this word. It's actually an older technology that was developed back in the mainframe days. Because if you think about what mainframe used, they used tapes. so this is still useful today because it works really still with any technology that uses magnetic technology to represent the zeros and the ones so we can use it for hard disk drives or any other media technology that uses magnetic technology to represent the zeros and the ones. All right, that's what degaussing is. And note where we've put it here, where the industry has put it. right it's right in between destruction and purging because typically by degaussing some sort of media like a tape for example you make that tape unusable, but you're not quite really destroying it by using a destruction method. That's why it sits in between destruction and purging, but that's a really good method still. All right, now the next one that we see here is encryption. Crypto erase or crypto shredding is what the industry refers this to as. And basically, there's a slide coming up that talks about this, but basically what crypto shredding or crypto erase is, is you encrypt your data and then listen to the words here because they are important. You securely and defensively destroy every copy of the key. In other words, you encrypt something and then destroy the keys so that that data cannot be decrypted because you've securely and defensively gotten rid of the keys. And so there's no way for that data to be reconstructed. That's why that fits. really in the purging category in here, and they've put it in between purging and clearing. All right. And here are some of the other options that you may have heard of, like overwriting and wiping and formatting even. and erasing deleting and so on these all fit in the clearing category which again is defined as may not allow that data to be reconstructed even overwriting which sometimes is the only option that you may have in certain architectures but again As far as we are concerned, and the exam is concerned, and the industry is concerned, any of these methods, including, for example, how many times you're going to overwrite your data, will always be dictated by the value of that data, obviously at the time of destruction, which is what we're talking about here. All right. And even formatting and low level formatting and so on. These are all clearing methods according to the industry. All right. So you may see some of the ones that you see up here. and where they actually fit as far as categories are concerned.
